items.subtopic: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Y. pestis as a Bioterrorism Agent
items.content: Yersinia pestis, the causative agent of plague, is considered a potential bioterrorism threat due to its high fatality rate and ability to spread via respiratory transmission.
keyPoints: Y. pestis can cause bubonic, septicemic, or pneumonic forms of plague.
Respiratory route is not the most common but poses significant risk for epidemic spread.
Legislation restricts access to Y. pestis due to its potential as a bioterrorism agent.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Epidemiology of Human Plague Outbreaks
items.content: Human plague outbreaks typically follow epizootics in rodent populations, with environmental factors potentially triggering these events.
keyPoints: Plague follows an outbreak (epizootic) in a host rodent population.
Mass deaths among rodents lead fleas to seek new hosts, spreading the infection.
Environmental factors may precipitate epizootics; research continues on enzootic reservoirs.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Understanding Plague Epidemiology: Regional Prevalence and Outbreak Patterns
items.content: Plague predominantly occurs in lightly populated regions of Africa, Asia, and the Americas. Between January 2013 and December 2018, there were 2886 reported cases globally with a case-fatality rate of 17%. Over 97% of these cases were from Africa, particularly Madagascar, which experienced an urban outbreak in 2017 involving over 2400 suspected cases, predominantly pneumonic plague (78%).
keyPoints: Plague enzootic areas: lightly populated regions of Africa, Asia, and the Americas.
Global case reports (2013-2018): 2886 cases with a 17% fatality rate.
Geographical distribution: Over 97% of cases in Africa; major outbreaks in Madagascar.
Madagascar 2017 outbreak: >2400 suspected cases, 78% pneumonic plague.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · GLOBAL FEATURES

items.title: Plague Transmission and Risk Factors in the United States
items.content: In the U.S., plague is enzootic on the western side from southwestern Canada to Mexico. Most cases occur in 'Four Corners' and parts of California, Oregon, and Nevada. From 1970-2020, there were 496 reported cases with an average of seven per year recently. Human contact with infected animals or fleas is a significant risk factor, occurring in at least 50% of cases.
keyPoints: U.S. enzootic regions: Western side from southwestern Canada to Mexico.
High-risk areas: 'Four Corners' and parts of California, Oregon, Nevada.
Reported U.S. cases (1970-2020): 496 total, average seven per year recently.
Risk factor: Prior animal contact in at least 50% of cases.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · GLOBAL FEATURES

items.title: Clinical Forms and Mortality Rates of Plague
items.content: Bubonic plague is the most common form reported worldwide, accounting for 82% of documented U.S. cases from 1900 to 2012. Pneumonic plague, though less common, is highly fatal; untreated pneumonic plague has a mortality rate of 98%, while treated cases have a death rate of 17%. Mortality in the U.S. post-antibiotics (1942 onward) dropped significantly.
keyPoints: Predominant form: Bubonic plague, 82% of documented U.S. cases.
Pneumonic plague mortality: 98% untreated, 17% treated globally.
U.S. case distribution (1900-2012): 10% septicemic, 8% pneumonic.
Postantibiotic era mortality in the U.S.: Dropped to 16%, compared to 66% pre-1942.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · GLOBAL FEATURES

items.title: Y. pestis Flea Transmission Mechanism
items.content: Y. pestis transmits from fleas to mammals through a process involving biofilm formation in the flea midgut, which can block blood feeding and lead to transmission during bites.
keyPoints: Biofilm-embedded Y. pestis blocks the proventriculus in some fleas, leading to transmission upon biting.
Phospholipase D (encoded by ymt gene) is required for colonization in fleas fed on infected blood from Rattus rattus but not Rattus norvegicus.
10 unblocked fleas are needed to infect a mammalian host.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Y. pestis Virulence Factors in Mammals
items.content: Y. pestis relies on specific plasmid-encoded factors for high virulence and dissemination within mammalian hosts.
keyPoints: Plasminogen activator Pla (encoded by pPCP1) is crucial for dissemination from the inoculation site, degradation of complement, and adherence to laminin.
Fimbrial capsule-forming protein (CaI or F1 antigen) enhances transmission efficiency in mouse models.
Type III secretion systems and F1 capsule prevent phagocytosis and promote bacterial survival.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Immune Evasion by Y. pestis
items.content: Y. pestis employs multiple strategies to evade the host immune response, allowing it to survive and replicate within phagocytic cells.
keyPoints: Avoids autophagy in macrophages and survives in neutrophils.
Type III secretion effectors block extrusion of microbicidal DNA by neutrophils and induce apoptotic cell death.
LcrV binding to FPR1 on phagocytes targets immune cells, while overproduction reduces inflammation.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Bubo Formation and Spread in Plague
items.content: Y. pestis causes bubo formation through replication in regional lymph nodes, leading to hemorrhagic or necrotic changes with bacterial infiltration and fibrin deposition.
keyPoints: Replication of Y. pestis results in local swelling known as a bubo.
Histology shows hemorrhagic or necrotic nodes with thrombosed vessels and bacteria replacing lymphoid cells.
Periglandular tissues are inflamed with serosanguineous, gelatinous exudate containing bacteria.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.title: Progression to Septicemia and Secondary Infections in Plague
items.content: Y. pestis spreads via lymphatics causing high-grade bacteremia, leading to septicemia and potential secondary infections like plague pneumonia.
keyPoints: Spread through lymphatic vessels results in second-order primary buboes.
Transient bacteremia can be detected initially; efferent lymphatics spread leads to high-grade bacteremia.
Hematogenous spread causes uncontrolled septicemia, potentially without prior bubo development (septicemic plague).
Secondary plague pneumonia occurs with bacteria more prominent in the interstitium.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.title: Key Features and Differential Diagnosis of Bubonic Plague
items.content: Bubonic plague presents suddenly after an incubation period of 2–6 days with fever (>38°C), malaise, myalgia, dizziness, and painful lymphadenitis. The characteristic bubo is tender, erythematous, and may have a boggy consistency with a hard core. It's often inguinal but can occur in other regions depending on the bite site.
keyPoints: Incubation period: 2–6 days
Symptoms include fever (>38°C), malaise, myalgia, dizziness
Lymphadenitis manifests as a tender bubo with boggy consistency and hard core
Most common location: inguinal; other sites: crural, axillary, cervical, submaxillary
Differential diagnosis includes streptococcal or staphylococcal infection, tularemia, cat-scratch disease, tick typhus, infectious mononucleosis, lymphatic filariasis
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Presentation and Risk Factors of Primary Septicemic Plague
items.content: Primary septicemic plague occurs in 10–25% of Y. pestis infections, presenting as gram-negative septicemia without preceding lymphadenopathy. It can occur at any age but is more common in persons >40 years old. Chronic conditions like diabetes mellitus and undiagnosed hemochromatosis increase risk.
keyPoints: Occurs in 10–25% of Y. pestis infections
Presentation: gram-negative septicemia without lymphadenopathy
Higher incidence in persons >40 years old
Risk factors include diabetes mellitus and undiagnosed hemochromatosis
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Characteristics of Primary Pneumonic Plague
items.content: Primary pneumonic plague results from inhaling infectious droplets, with a short incubation period (1–7 days). It presents suddenly with fever, headache, myalgia, and respiratory symptoms like cough and dyspnea. Chest x-ray shows bilateral alveolar infiltrates, and it poses bioterrorism concerns due to potential aerosolized transmission.
keyPoints: Incubation period: 1–7 days
Symptoms include fever, headache, myalgia, cough, dyspnea
Chest x-ray shows bilateral alveolar infiltrates
Concerns about bioterrorism due to aerosolized transmission
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | And | Probable Case | plus 1 of the following | Confirmed Case | Plus at least 1 of the following
items.title: Diagnosing Plague: From Presumptive to Confirmed Cases
items.content: In regions where Y. pestis is prevalent, initial presumptive diagnosis followed by reference laboratory confirmation is recommended due to limited local lab facilities. In the U.S., a suspected case becomes probable with epidemiological context and one of several diagnostic criteria, such as detection of F1 antigen or direct microscopy showing bipolar staining. A confirmed case requires isolation of Y. pestis from clinical samples or evidence of seroconversion.
keyPoints: Initial presumptive diagnosis followed by reference laboratory confirmation is recommended in endemic regions.
Probable case: Epidemiological context plus one diagnostic criterion (e.g., F1 antigen detection, direct microscopy showing bipolar staining).
Confirmed case: Isolation of Y. pestis or seroconversion with a fourfold difference in anti-F1 antibody titer.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | And | Probable Case | plus 1 of the following | Confirmed Case | Plus at least 1 of the following

items.subtopic: LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | And | Probable Case | plus 1 of the following | Confirmed Case | Plus at least 1 of the following
items.title: Criteria for Confirming Y. pestis Infection
items.content: A confirmed case of plague requires meeting the suspected case definition and additional criteria such as isolation of Y. pestis from clinical samples, which involves specific tests like phage lysis and F1 antigen detection, or seroconversion evidenced by a fourfold increase in anti-F1 antibody titer.
keyPoints: Isolation of Y. pestis requires appropriate colonial morphology and positive results for at least two tests: phage lysis at 20-25°C and 37°C, biochemical profile, F1 antigen detection.
Seroconversion is confirmed by a fourfold difference in anti-F1 antibody titer in paired serum samples drawn at least 2 weeks apart.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | And | Probable Case | plus 1 of the following | Confirmed Case | Plus at least 1 of the following

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Suspected Yersinia pestis Infection
items.content: When diagnosing suspected Y. pestis infection, at least two laboratory tests should be conducted and found negative to rule out the disease. If confirmatory tests are unavailable, two negative rapid antigen tests for F1 antigen on specimens collected 24 hours apart can exclude plague.
keyPoints: At least two of these tests must be negative: rapid antigen test or direct immunofluorescence against F1 antigen, direct microscopy, convalescent serology, culture, PCR
Two negative rapid antigen tests for F1 antigen on 2 clinical specimens collected at 24-h intervals can exclude plague in the absence of confirmatory tests
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 1)

items.title: Identification and Culture Characteristics of Y. pestis
items.content: Y. pestis is a gram-negative coccobacillus with unique characteristics that aid in its identification. It appears bipolar under staining, lacks motility at body temperature, and grows optimally below 37°C.
keyPoints: Gram-negative coccobacilli, 1–3 µm in length and 0.5–0.8 µm in diameter
Bipolar appearance (closed safety pin) with Wayson or Wright-Giemsa stain
Nonmotile at 37°C; motile at 25°C
Optimal growth temperature is <37°C, forms pinpoint colonies on SBA at 24 h
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 1)

items.title: Use and Efficacy of F1 Rapid Diagnostic Test in Plague Diagnosis
items.content: The F1RDT is a rapid diagnostic test for Y. pestis that detects the F1 antigen, showing high sensitivity for bubonic or pneumonic plague diagnosis. However, its specificity for pneumonic plague diagnosed by culture is 71%.
keyPoints: F1RDT detects F1 antigen produced in large amounts by Y. pestis
Effective for laboratory and near-patient use on bubo aspirates and sputum
At least as sensitive as laboratory culture for diagnosing bubonic or pneumonic plague
Specificity of 71% for pneumonic plague diagnosed by culture
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic PCR for Y. pestis
items.content: The WHO mandates that diagnostic PCR or RT-PCR assays must show positivity for at least two different targets specific to Y. pestis, including plasmid-based genes like caf1 and pla, along with three chromosomal genes.
keyPoints: Diagnostic PCR/RT-PCR assays require positivity for at least two different targets specific for Y. pestis.
Targets include the plasmid-based caf1 (F1 antigen) and pla (plasminogen activator) genes, plus three chromosomal genes.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 2)

items.title: Laboratory Findings in Plague
items.content: In plague cases, WBC counts are generally elevated to 10,000–20,000/µL with neutrophilic leukocytosis and a left shift. However, normal or low counts can occur, and very high counts (>100,000/µL) may be seen in children.
keyPoints: WBC count typically raised to 10,000–20,000/µL with neutrophilic leukocytosis and a left shift.
Normal or low WBC counts can occur; very high counts (>100,000/µL) may be seen in children.
Elevated fibrinogen degradation products are common; platelet counts usually normal or low-normal.
Disseminated intravascular coagulation occurs in a significant minority of patients.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 2)

items.title: Antimicrobial Therapy for Plague: Choosing the Right Agent
items.content: The treatment of plague involves a 10- to 14-day course of antimicrobials or until 2 days after fever subsides. Streptomycin is traditionally preferred, but gentamicin is recommended by some due to its safety and efficacy profile. Fluoroquinolones like levofloxacin, moxifloxacin, and ciprofloxacin are FDA-approved for treatment under the Animal Rule and are included in WHO guidelines as first-line treatments.
keyPoints: 10- to 14-day antimicrobial therapy recommended
Streptomycin is historically preferred but has adverse reactions and limited availability
Gentamicin is safe and effective, recommended over streptomycin by some experts
Levofloxacin, moxifloxacin, ciprofloxacin approved under Animal Rule for treatment and prophylaxis
WHO 2021 guidelines recommend fluoroquinolones as first-line treatments
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague

items.title: Doxycycline's Role in Plague Treatment: Efficacy and Safety
items.content: Doxycycline is the preferred tetracycline for plague treatment, effective orally at 100 mg twice daily. It matches intramuscular gentamicin efficacy (2.5 mg/kg twice daily) without causing dental staining in children, unlike other tetracyclines.
keyPoints: Doxycycline is preferred over other tetracyclines for plague
Effective at 100 mg orally twice daily
Comparable efficacy to gentamicin (2.5 mg/kg twice daily)
Does not cause dental staining in children
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague

items.title: Risk Reduction in Endemic Areas
items.content: Prevention of plague in endemic areas focuses on minimizing contact with infected fleas and droplets from humans or animals. Key strategies include surveillance, personal protective measures, and rodent control.
keyPoints: Surveillance for Y. pestis among animal hosts and vectors is essential.
Avoidance of areas with identified plague epizootics.
Use of gloves when handling wild-animal carcasses in endemic regions.
Personal protective measures: insect repellent, insecticide, protective clothing.
Rodent-proofing buildings and removing potential habitats like woodpiles.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 1)

items.title: Management of Pneumonic Plague
items.content: Patients suspected of pneumonic plague require isolation with droplet precautions until pneumonia is excluded or treated for 48 hours. Protective equipment includes N95 masks, gowns, gloves, and face shields.
keyPoints: Isolation with negative pressure if available.
Droplet precautions for 48 hours post-effective antimicrobial therapy.
Use of fit-tested N95 mask, gown, gloves, and face shield or goggles.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 1)

items.title: Postexposure Antimicrobial Prophylaxis
items.content: Following close contact with untreated pneumonic plague patients, a 7-day course of antimicrobial prophylaxis is recommended. Levofloxacin and ciprofloxacin are preferred over doxycycline for higher survival rates.
keyPoints: Antimicrobial prophylaxis lasts 7 days after close contact (<2 m) with untreated pneumonic plague.
Levofloxacin and ciprofloxacin have higher survival rates than doxycycline in animal studies.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Plague Vaccine Development and Regulatory Pathways
items.content: The development of plague vaccines, particularly the rF1-V subunit vaccine, is progressing with promising results in animal models. The FDA evaluates these vaccines under the Animal Rule due to challenges in conducting human efficacy trials. This rule allows for the use of animal study data combined with immune response indicators from humans.
keyPoints: rF1-V subunit vaccine shows promise in protecting mice and nonhuman primates against bubonic and pneumonic plague.
FDA assesses vaccines under the Animal Rule, utilizing efficacy data from animals and human immune correlates.
The rF1-V vaccine has orphan drug status.
WHO's Target Product Profile aids phase 3 trial design for prioritizing vaccine candidates.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 2) | YERSINIOSIS

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Yersiniosis
items.content: Yersiniosis, primarily caused by Y. enterocolitica or Y. pseudotuberculosis, often presents with abdominal pain and can mimic appendicitis. Diagnosis has shifted from culture to PCR systems for Y. enterocolitica, though not yet for Y. pseudotuberculosis.
keyPoints: Yersiniosis is a zoonotic infection common in children and colder climates.
Symptoms include abdominal pain and diarrhea (absent in up to 50% of cases).
Y. enterocolitica often causes terminal ileitis, while Y. pseudotuberculosis can cause mesenteric adenitis.
Diagnosis is increasingly based on PCR systems for gastrointestinal infections, though not all species are covered.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 2) | YERSINIOSIS

items.title: Yersiniosis Epidemiology: Risk Factors and Common Sources
items.content: Y. enterocolitica is a widespread pathogen linked to various animals, environmental samples, and food products. It's the third most common bacterial food-borne zoonosis in Europe, notably prevalent in Germany and Scandinavia. Children under 4 are more prone to diarrhea, while older children and adults often experience abdominal pain with mesenteric adenitis and terminal ileitis. Risk factors for severe disease include preexisting conditions like diabetes mellitus, liver disease, iron overload disorders, advanced age, malignancy, or HIV/AIDS.
keyPoints: Y. enterocolitica is the predominant cause of yersiniosis, accounting for >99% of cases.
Most common in Europe, especially Germany and Scandinavia.
Children <4 years more likely to present with diarrhea; older children/adults often have abdominal pain with mesenteric adenitis and terminal ileitis.
Septicemia risk is higher in patients with preexisting conditions such as diabetes mellitus, liver disease, iron overload (e.g., thalassemia, hemochromatosis), advanced age, malignancy, or HIV/AIDS.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Transmission and Outbreak Sources of Yersiniosis
items.content: Y. enterocolitica is often transmitted through the consumption of raw or undercooked pork products, as well as contaminated milk and vegetables like spinach. Person-to-person transmission is less common compared to other gastrointestinal pathogens. Contact with companion animals has been identified as a risk factor for children in Sweden. Y. pseudotuberculosis, while less frequently reported, can cause outbreaks linked to foods such as lettuce, raw carrots, tomatoes, or unpasteurized milk.
keyPoints: Raw pork products are strongly linked to Y. enterocolitica infection due to high prevalence of pathogenic strains in pigs.
Outbreaks associated with contaminated milk (pasteurized and unpasteurized) and vegetables like spinach.
Person-to-person transmission is rare compared to other gastrointestinal infections.
Contact with companion animals is a risk factor for Y. enterocolitica infection among children in Sweden.
Y. pseudotuberculosis outbreaks linked to foods such as lettuce, raw carrots, tomatoes, or unpasteurized milk.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Mechanism of Yersinia Invasion via M Cells
items.content: Yersinia species, including Y. enterocolitica and Y. pseudotuberculosis, initially replicate in the small intestine after oral infection and invade Peyer's patches through M cells. This process is facilitated by nonfimbrial adhesins such as invasin (Inv) and YadA, which interact with host cell integrins and extracellular matrix proteins.
keyPoints: Initial replication occurs in the small intestine following oral infection.
Invasion of Peyer's patches involves M cells via adhesins like Inv and YadA.
Inv interacts with $ \beta1 $ integrins on M cells, while YadA binds to collagen and fibronectin.
Only a small proportion of bacteria invade tissues; host restricts invasion in previously infected Peyer's patches.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Role of Yersinia Outer Proteins (Yops) in Immune Evasion
items.content: The type III secretion system encoded by the pYV plasmid injects Yersinia outer proteins (Yops) into host immune cells, altering innate immune responses. This results in reduced phagocytosis and reactive oxygen species production, as well as apoptosis of macrophages.
keyPoints: Type III secretion system delivers Yops to host macrophages, neutrophils, and dendritic cells.
Yops disrupt signal transduction pathways, reducing phagocytosis and oxidative burst in neutrophils.
Apoptosis is induced in macrophages by Yops.
YadA facilitates targeting of immune cells for Yop delivery.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Y. enterocolitica Pseudoappendicitis: Recognizing and Managing to Avoid Unnecessary Surgery
items.content: Infections with Y. enterocolitica can mimic appendicitis, particularly in children under 4 years old, leading to unnecessary surgical interventions like laparotomy or appendectomy. It's crucial for clinicians to recognize the signs of pseudoappendicitis caused by Yersinia infection.
keyPoints: Self-limiting diarrhea is common in Y. enterocolitica infections, especially in children <4 years.
Pseudoappendicitis often presents with abdominal pain localized to the right iliac fossa.
Appendectomy is not indicated for pseudoappendicitis due to Yersinia infection.
Endoscopy and ultrasound may show thickening of the terminal ileum and cecum, along with enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Y. pseudotuberculosis Infection: Recognizing Systemic Complications and Associations with Kawasaki Disease
items.content: Y. pseudotuberculosis can present with abdominal pain and fever, and in certain regions, it is associated with illnesses resembling Kawasaki disease due to a superantigenic toxin. Awareness of these associations is critical for proper diagnosis and management.
keyPoints: Y. pseudotuberculosis infection often presents as abdominal pain and fever rather than diarrhea.
The Y. pseudotuberculosis mitogen (YPM) is linked with Far Eastern scarlet-like fever, which shares similarities with Kawasaki disease.
Epidemiologic links suggest an association between exposure to superantigen-positive Y. pseudotuberculosis and elevated incidence of Kawasaki disease.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Reactive Arthritis Post-Yersinia Infection: Mechanism and Prevalence
items.content: Reactive arthritis following Yersinia infection is due to autoimmune activity triggered by bacterial components deposited in joints, not viable bacteria. This condition typically develops 2–4 weeks post-infection and is often associated with HLA-B27 positivity.
keyPoints: Reactive arthritis occurs within 2–4 weeks of a preceding Yersinia infection.
Most cases are linked to HLA-B27 positivity.
Y. enterocolitica is the most common cause, but Y. pseudotuberculosis-associated reactive arthritis is notable in Finland with 12% developing it post-infection.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · POSTINFECTIVE PHENOMENA

items.title: Association Between Yersinia Infection and Thyroid Disorders
items.content: There is an association between antithyroid antibodies in Graves' disease and prior Y. enterocolitica infection, potentially due to cross-reactivity with a TSH-binding site on the bacterium.
keyPoints: Antibody evidence of prior Y. enterocolitica infection has been noted in Graves' disease patients since the 1970s.
Y. enterocolitica contains a TSH-binding site recognized by antibodies from Graves' disease patients.
The significance of this cross-reactivity in the etiology of Graves' disease remains unclear.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · POSTINFECTIVE PHENOMENA

items.title: Optimal Conditions for Culturing Yersinia Species
items.content: Yersinia species can be isolated using standard laboratory culture methods. For stool or nonsterile samples, selective media like CIN agar are used, with pre-enrichment in broth or phosphate-buffered saline at 4°C or 16°C.
keyPoints: Use of CIN agar for culturing Yersinia from stool or nonsterile samples
Pre-enrichment in broth or phosphate-buffered saline at 4°C or 16°C
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | Yersiniosis

items.title: PCR and MALDI-TOF for Yersinia Detection
items.content: Multiplex PCR screens can detect Y. enterocolitica-specific DNA in feces, increasing detection rates. MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry can speciate Y. enterocolitica and Y. pseudotuberculosis but cannot differentiate Y. pestis from Y. pseudotuberculosis.
keyPoints: Multiplex PCR screens detect Y. enterocolitica-specific DNA in feces
MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry speciates Y. enterocolitica and Y. pseudotuberculosis
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | Yersiniosis

items.title: Serologic Diagnosis of Yersinia Infections
items.content: Agglutinating or ELISA antibody titers to specific O-antigen types are used for retrospective diagnosis. IgA and IgG antibodies persist in patients with reactive arthritis, and serologic cross-reactions can occur between Y. enterocolitica serogroup O:9 and Brucella.
keyPoints: Use of agglutinating or ELISA antibody titers for retrospective diagnosis
Persistence of IgA and IgG antibodies in reactive arthritis
Serologic cross-reactions between Y. enterocolitica serogroup O:9 and Brucella
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | Yersiniosis

items.subtopic: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: Which form of plague is NOT the most common route of transmission?
items.explanation: The respiratory route, while concerning for epidemic spread, is not the most common route of plague transmission.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: What is a distinguishing feature of Y. pestis when grown in vivo or at 37°C?
items.explanation: Y. pestis forms an amorphous capsule made from a plasmid-specified fimbrial protein, known as F1 antigen.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: What is the primary reservoir for Y. pestis during enzootic plague?
items.explanation: The primary reservoirs are rodent populations, which can vary between regions.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which region reported the highest number of plague cases from January 2013 to December 2018?
items.explanation: More than 97% of the global plague cases reported during this period were in Africa.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · GLOBAL FEATURES

items.question: What was the predominant form of plague reported worldwide?
items.explanation: Worldwide, bubonic plague is the predominant form reported.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · GLOBAL FEATURES

items.question: Which flea species is most efficient in transmitting plague among rats and humans?
items.explanation: The oriental rat flea Xenopsylla cheopis is the most efficient vector for transmission of plague among rats and onward to humans.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · GLOBAL FEATURES

items.question: What was the global case-fatality rate for plague from January 2013 to December 2018?
items.explanation: The global case-fatality rate for plague during this period was 17%.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · GLOBAL FEATURES

items.question: Which gene acquisition allowed Y. pestis to form biofilm-embedded aggregates in the flea midgut?
options: Presence of a virulence plasmid, pYV
items.explanation: Biofilm synthesis requires the chromosomal hms locus and phospholipase D encoded by the ymt gene.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: What is essential for Y. pestis to cause high-level virulence in mice models simulating fleabites?
options: Phospholipase D encoded by the ymt gene
items.explanation: Plasminogen activator Pla is essential for high-level virulence in mice models simulating fleabites.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: Which gene's absence from the pFra (pMT1) plasmid suggests Y. pestis was a common fatal human infection before flea-borne transmission was fully optimized?
items.explanation: The absence of the ymt gene in some ancient Y. pestis sequences suggests it was a common fatal human infection before flea-borne transmission.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: What is the role of LcrV in Y. pestis pathogenesis?
options: It binds to FPR1 on phagocytic cells.
items.explanation: LcrV binds to the N-formylpeptide receptor (FPR1) on phagocytic cells, aiding in immune cell targeting and exerting an anti-inflammatory effect.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: What is a characteristic histological finding in a lymph node affected by Y. pestis infection?
options: The lymphoid cells and normal architecture are replaced by large numbers of bacteria and fibrin.
The lymph node shows increased production of lymphocytes without bacterial presence.
There is an absence of hemorrhagic or necrotic changes in the lymph node.
The lymph node exhibits a reduction in fibrin deposition with no bacterial infiltration.
items.explanation: Histologically, Y. pestis infection leads to replacement of lymphoid cells and normal architecture by bacteria and fibrin.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.question: What is the consequence of hematogenous spread of Y. pestis to the lungs?
options: Primary plague pneumonia with bacteria more prominent in air spaces.
Secondary plague pneumonia with bacteria initially more prominent in the interstitium.
Complete absence of bacterial presence in lung tissues.
Formation of primary buboes within pulmonary lymph nodes.
items.explanation: Hematogenous spread to the lungs results in secondary plague pneumonia, with bacteria initially more prominent in the interstitium.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.question: What can be detected during the initial phase of Y. pestis infection?
options: Immediate septicemic plague without bubo development.
Absence of any detectable bacteria in lymph nodes.
items.explanation: Initially, transient bacteremia can be detected during Y. pestis infection before progression to high-grade bacteremia.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.question: Which lymph node region is most commonly affected by buboes in bubonic plague?
items.explanation: The inguinal region is the most common site for buboes in bubonic plague.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What differentiates bubonic plague from other causes of acute focal lymphadenopathy?
options: Rapid progression and absence of visible cellulitis
Slower progression with visible ascending lymphangitis
items.explanation: Bubonic plague progresses rapidly, is very painful, and lacks visible cellulitis or ascending lymphangitis.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What is a potential consequence of untreated bubonic plague?
items.explanation: Without treatment, Y. pestis can disseminate and cause secondary pneumonic plague, leading to person-to-person transmission.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which condition is associated with an increased risk of septicemic plague?
items.explanation: Diabetes mellitus and undiagnosed hemochromatosis are associated with an increased risk of septicemic plague.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | And | Probable Case | plus 1 of the following | Confirmed Case | Plus at least 1 of the following
items.question: Which one of these is NOT a method for presumptive diagnosis of Y. pestis infection?
options: F1 antigen detected in bubo aspirate, sputum, blood, or postmortem tissues by rapid antigen test or direct immunofluorescence
A single anti-F1 serology without evidence of previous Yersinia pestis infection or immunization
Direct microscopy in a clinical sample: gram-negative coccobacilli that display bipolar staining with Wayson or Wright-Giemsa stain
Isolation of Y. pestis from a clinical sample
items.explanation: Isolation of Y. pestis is used for confirmed diagnosis, not presumptive.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | And | Probable Case | plus 1 of the following | Confirmed Case | Plus at least 1 of the following

items.subtopic: LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | And | Probable Case | plus 1 of the following | Confirmed Case | Plus at least 1 of the following
items.question: Which test is NOT part of confirming Y. pestis isolation in a clinical sample?
options: Phage lysis of cultures at $20-25^{	ext{o}}$C and $37^{	ext{o}}$C
items.explanation: Species-specific PCR is used, not non-specific bacterial DNA.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | And | Probable Case | plus 1 of the following | Confirmed Case | Plus at least 1 of the following

items.subtopic: LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | And | Probable Case | plus 1 of the following | Confirmed Case | Plus at least 1 of the following
items.question: What indicates a confirmed case of Y. pestis infection?
options: Meeting the definition of a suspected case and detection of F1 antigen by rapid test
Isolation of Y. pestis from a clinical sample with appropriate tests positive
A single anti-F1 serology without evidence of previous infection or immunization
Epidemiological context suggesting possible exposure to plague
items.explanation: Confirmed case requires isolation of Y. pestis and additional confirmatory tests.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | And | Probable Case | plus 1 of the following | Confirmed Case | Plus at least 1 of the following

items.question: Which characteristic distinguishes Y. pestis from other Yersinia species?
items.explanation: Y. pestis is distinguished from other Yersinia species by its lack of motility.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 1)

items.question: What is the optimal growth temperature for Y. pestis on nutrient agar?
items.explanation: The optimal growth temperature for Y. pestis is <37°C, specifically 25–29°C.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 1)

items.question: Which medium can be useful for culturing contaminated specimens such as sputum?
items.explanation: Yersinia-specific CIN (cefsulodin, triclosan [Irgasan], novobiocin) agar is useful for culturing contaminated specimens.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 1)

items.question: What does a positive F1RDT indicate in the context of diagnosing plague?
options: Negative predictive value for bubonic plague
items.explanation: A positive F1RDT indicates a probable case requiring treatment, but additional tests are needed to confirm pneumonic plague.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 1)

items.question: Which two targets are required by WHO diagnostic PCR assays for Y. pestis?
options: pla gene and three other chromosomal genes
items.explanation: WHO requires positivity for at least two different targets, including the plasmid-based caf1 and pla genes.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 2)

items.question: What type of specimens can be used with the FDA-authorized Biofire FilmArray NGDS Warrior Panel?
options: whole blood, blood cultures, and sputum
items.explanation: The panel is suitable for use with whole blood (EDTA), blood cultures, and sputum specimens.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 2)

items.question: What can be used to make a retrospective serologic diagnosis in the absence of other positive tests?
options: rising titers of hemagglutinating antibody to F1 antigen
ELISA for IgA antibodies to F1 antigen
items.explanation: Rising titers of hemagglutinating antibody to F1 antigen can be used for retrospective serologic diagnosis.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 2)

items.question: Which antibiotic is historically the parenteral treatment of choice for plague according to FDA approval?
items.explanation: Streptomycin has historically been the parenteral treatment of choice for plague and is approved by the FDA.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague

items.question: Which antibiotic is recommended over streptomycin due to its adverse-reaction profile and limited availability?
items.explanation: Some experts now recommend gentamicin over streptomycin due to its adverse-reaction profile and limited availability.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague

items.question: Which fluoroquinolone is recommended by the WHO for treating plague meningitis?
items.explanation: The WHO recommends moxifloxacin and ofloxacin for plague meningitis.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague

items.question: Which of the following is a recommended personal protective measure to avoid rodent fleabite during outdoor activity in plague-endemic areas?
options: Use of insect repellent, insecticide, and protective clothing
items.explanation: General measures to avoid rodent fleabite include the use of insect repellent, insecticide, and protective clothing.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 1)

items.question: What is a key step that must precede any attempt to reduce rodent numbers in plague-endemic areas?
items.explanation: Any attempt to reduce rodent numbers must be preceded by flea suppression.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 1)

items.question: Which type of mask is recommended for personal protective equipment during potential aerosol-generating procedures in plague-endemic areas?
items.explanation: WHO guidance recommends a fit-tested N95 face mask for potential aerosol-generating procedures.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 1)

items.question: Following close contact with an untreated pneumonic plague patient, what is the recommended duration of postexposure antimicrobial prophylaxis?
items.explanation: Postexposure antimicrobial prophylaxis lasting 7 days is recommended following close contact with untreated pneumonic plague.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 1)

items.question: Which vaccine candidate for plague is close to licensing and has been evaluated in phase 2 clinical trials?
items.explanation: The rF1-V subunit vaccine is close to licensing and has been evaluated in phase 2 clinical trials.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 2) | YERSINIOSIS

items.question: What rule will the FDA use to assess plague vaccines for human use due to difficulties with phase 3 trials?
items.explanation: The FDA will use the Animal Rule to assess plague vaccines for human use.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 2) | YERSINIOSIS

items.question: Which organism is more closely associated with terminal ileitis in yersiniosis?
items.explanation: Y. enterocolitica is more closely associated with terminal ileitis.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 2) | YERSINIOSIS

items.question: Which factor is most strongly linked with infection by pathogenic Y. enterocolitica strains?
options: Consumption of raw or processed pork products
items.explanation: The passage states that consumption or preparation of raw and some processed pork products is strongly linked with infection due to a high percentage of pigs carrying pathogenic Y. enterocolitica strains.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which group of individuals is more likely to present with septicemia from Y. enterocolitica?
options: Individuals with preexisting conditions such as diabetes mellitus or liver disease
Elderly individuals without any preexisting conditions
items.explanation: The passage indicates that septicemia is more likely in patients with preexisting conditions like diabetes mellitus, liver disease, or iron overload.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following statements about Y. pseudotuberculosis infection presentation is correct?
options: It commonly presents as diarrhea in children.
It often results in erythema nodosum.
It typically presents with fever and abdominal pain due to mesenteric lymphadenitis.
It is frequently identified from stool culture isolates.
items.explanation: The passage states that Y. pseudotuberculosis infection more likely presents as fever and abdominal pain due to mesenteric lymphadenitis, often identified from blood cultures.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following adhesins is chromosomally encoded in Yersinia species?
items.explanation: Invasin (Inv) is chromosomally encoded, whereas YadA is plasmid-encoded.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What is the role of YadA in Y. pseudotuberculosis?
options: It binds to enterocytes and facilitates invasion.
It interacts with extracellular matrix proteins like collagen and fibronectin.
It inhibits phagocytosis by binding to host cell surfaces.
It is responsible for the production of reactive oxygen species.
items.explanation: YadA in Y. pseudotuberculosis interacts with extracellular matrix proteins such as collagen and fibronectin, facilitating invasion.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: How does the pYV plasmid-encoded type III secretion system affect host immune cells?
options: It triggers apoptosis of neutrophils and dendritic cells.
It reduces production of reactive oxygen species in neutrophils.
It increases the binding of complement regulators to bacteria.
items.explanation: The type III secretion system injects toxins into host immune cells, reducing phagocytosis and inhibiting production of reactive oxygen species by neutrophils.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which clinical feature is most commonly associated with Y. enterocolitica infection in children under 4 years of age?
items.explanation: Self-limiting diarrhea is the most common presentation in young children infected with Y. enterocolitica.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What condition may lead to unnecessary laparotomy for presumed appendicitis due to Yersinia infection?
items.explanation: Y. enterocolitica can cause pseudoappendicitis, often leading to unnecessary laparotomy.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which condition is associated with the production of Y. pseudotuberculosis mitogen (YPM) in eastern Russia?
items.explanation: Y. pseudotuberculosis mitogen (YPM) is associated with Far Eastern scarlet-like fever in eastern Russia.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which HLA type is most commonly associated with reactive arthritis following Yersinia infection?
items.explanation: The majority of individuals affected by reactive arthritis due to Yersinia are HLA-B27 positive.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · POSTINFECTIVE PHENOMENA

items.question: Which species of Yersinia is most commonly associated with post-infective reactive arthritis?
items.explanation: Most Yersinia-associated cases of reactive arthritis follow Y. enterocolitica infection.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · POSTINFECTIVE PHENOMENA

items.question: Which condition occurs after Yersinia infection without evidence of HLA-B27 linkage?
items.explanation: Erythema nodosum occurs after Yersinia infection with no evidence of HLA-B27 linkage.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · POSTINFECTIVE PHENOMENA

items.question: Which method is commonly used for isolating yersinia from stool samples?
options: Culture on CIN agar with pre-enrichment in broth or phosphate-buffered saline at 4°C or 16°C
Direct PCR without any culture methods
MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry without prior culturing
items.explanation: Culture on CIN agar with pre-enrichment is the basis for isolating yersinia from stool samples.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | Yersiniosis

items.question: What limitation does MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry have in speciating Yersinia species?
options: It cannot separate Y. pestis from Y. pseudotuberculosis.
It cannot detect any Yersinia species.
It is unable to culture Yersinia species.
It does not work at low temperatures.
items.explanation: MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry can speciate isolates but cannot separate Y. pestis from Y. pseudotuberculosis.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | Yersiniosis

items.question: Which of the following is true about the detection of Y. enterocolitica in food samples?
options: There is no standard for PCR detection.
A standard from the International Organization for Standardization exists.
Detection is only possible through culture methods.
FDA-approved kits are not used for this purpose.
items.explanation: A standard for PCR detection of pathogenic Y. enterocolitica in food samples is available from the International Organization for Standardization.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | Yersiniosis

items.question: Why are multiple assays required to cover predominant serogroups of Yersinia?
options: Due to the similarity in lipopolysaccharide structures causing cross-reactions.
They can only be detected through MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry.
There are no assays available for these serogroups.
items.explanation: Multiple assays are required due to serologic cross-reactions between Y. enterocolitica serogroup O:9 and Brucella caused by similar lipopolysaccharide structures.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | Yersiniosis

items.subtopic: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: Y. pestis evolved from Yersinia pseudotuberculosis.
items.explanation: Taxonomic and paleogenomic evidence shows Y. pestis recently evolved from Yersinia pseudotuberculosis.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: The most common route of plague transmission is respiratory.
items.explanation: Respiratory transmission, while concerning for epidemics, is not the most common route.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Plague is enzootic on the eastern side of North America.
items.explanation: Plague is presently enzootic on the western side of North America.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · GLOBAL FEATURES

items.statement: The majority of plague cases in the United States from 1970 to 2020 occurred between May and October.
items.explanation: Most human cases in the U.S. occur from May to October, when people are outdoors and rodents and their fleas are most plentiful.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · GLOBAL FEATURES

items.statement: The ymt gene is required for Y. pestis to infect fleas fed on blood from the brown rat.
items.explanation: Y. pestis does not require an intact ymt gene to infect and be transmitted from fleas fed on blood from the brown rat.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.statement: Plasminogen activator Pla is encoded by the pYV plasmid.
items.explanation: Plasminogen activator Pla is encoded by the small pPCP1 (pPst) plasmid, not pYV.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.statement: Infection with Y. pestis is initially contained within the regional lymph nodes.
items.explanation: Y. pestis infection begins in and is initially contained within the infected regional lymph nodes.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.statement: Hematogenous spread of Y. pestis does not lead to secondary buboes.
items.explanation: Hematogenous spread results in secondary buboes as the infection progresses.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.statement: Bubonic plague can present without any visible signs if the infection involves intraabdominal nodes.
items.explanation: Abdominal pain from intraabdominal node involvement can occur without other visible signs.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Meningeal plague is the most common presentation of plague.
items.explanation: Meningeal plague occurs in ≤6% of reported cases, making it uncommon.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | And | Probable Case | plus 1 of the following | Confirmed Case | Plus at least 1 of the following
items.statement: The WHO recommends an initial presumptive diagnosis for Y. pestis infection.
items.explanation: Due to scarcity of laboratory facilities in endemic regions, WHO suggests a presumptive diagnosis followed by confirmation.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | And | Probable Case | plus 1 of the following | Confirmed Case | Plus at least 1 of the following

items.subtopic: LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | And | Probable Case | plus 1 of the following | Confirmed Case | Plus at least 1 of the following
items.statement: Direct microscopy alone is sufficient for confirming Y. pestis infection.
items.explanation: Direct microscopy can suggest probable case but not confirm; isolation or seroconversion is needed.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | And | Probable Case | plus 1 of the following | Confirmed Case | Plus at least 1 of the following

items.statement: Y. pestis is motile at 25°C.
items.explanation: Y. pestis lacks motility, distinguishing it from other Yersinia species which are motile at 25°C.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 1)

items.statement: F1RDT has a high negative predictive value for diagnosing plague.
items.explanation: F1RDT is noted to have a high negative predictive value, useful when other tests are unavailable.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 1)

items.statement: The WBC count is always elevated in plague.
items.explanation: WBC count can be normal or even low; leukopenia may develop.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 2)

items.statement: Disseminated intravascular coagulation in plague is characterized by elevated platelet counts.
items.explanation: It is characterized by low platelet counts, among other features.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 2)

items.statement: Chloramphenicol is recommended as a first-line therapy for all forms of plague.
items.explanation: Chloramphenicol is not generally recommended as first-line therapy but may be used for some focal complications due to its tissue penetration properties.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague

items.statement: Doxycycline can cause dental staining in children.
items.explanation: Recent evidence suggests that doxycycline does not cause dental staining in children because it binds calcium less readily than other tetracyclines.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague

items.statement: A live attenuated vaccine based on strain EV76 has no significant side effects.
items.explanation: The live attenuated vaccine based on strain EV76 is still used but has significant side effects.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 1)

items.statement: Flea control by insecticide treatment of wild rodents can minimize human contact with plague.
items.explanation: Flea control by insecticide treatment is effective in minimizing human contact with plague.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 1)

items.statement: The WHO has produced a Target Product Profile for phase 3 trial design and prioritization of plague vaccine candidates.
items.explanation: The WHO has indeed produced a TPP for phase 3 trial design and prioritization.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 2) | YERSINIOSIS

items.statement: Y. pseudotuberculosis is more likely to cause systemic infection in patients without diabetes or iron overload.
items.explanation: Systemic infection with Y. pseudotuberculosis is more likely in patients with diabetes or iron overload.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 2) | YERSINIOSIS

items.statement: Yersiniosis is the most common bacterial food-borne zoonosis reported in Europe.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that yersiniosis remains the third most common, not the most common, bacterial food-borne zoonosis reported in Europe.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Person-to-person transmission is a common mode of Y. enterocolitica infection.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that person-to-person transmission is much less likely with Y. enterocolitica than with other gastrointestinal infections.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: YadA in Y. enterocolitica binds fibronectin.
items.explanation: YadA of Y. enterocolitica lacks a crucial N-terminal region and does not bind fibronectin.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Spread to the liver and spleen in mice occurs independently of regional lymph node colonization.
items.explanation: Spread of Y. pseudotuberculosis and Y. enterocolitica to the liver and spleen occurs independently of regional lymph node colonization.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Appendectomy is indicated for Yersinia infection causing pseudoappendicitis.
items.explanation: Appendectomy is not indicated for Yersinia infection causing pseudoappendicitis.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Y. pseudotuberculosis infection commonly presents with diarrhea in children.
items.explanation: Y. pseudotuberculosis more likely presents as abdominal pain and fever than diarrhea.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Y. pseudotuberculosis-associated reactive arthritis is more common in Finland than other countries.
items.explanation: Y. pseudotuberculosis-associated reactive arthritis is well documented in Finland where infections are more common.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · POSTINFECTIVE PHENOMENA

items.statement: Graves' disease has a confirmed etiological link with Yersinia infection.
items.explanation: It remains unclear whether the cross-reactivity between antithyroid and anti-Yersinia antibodies is significant in the etiology of Graves' disease.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · POSTINFECTIVE PHENOMENA

items.statement: FDA-approved kits for enteric pathogens can detect both Y. enterocolitica and Y. pseudotuberculosis.
items.explanation: These kits generally do not detect Y. pseudotuberculosis.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | Yersiniosis

items.statement: Whole genome DNA sequencing can differentiate Y. enterocolitica biotypes.
items.explanation: Whole genome DNA sequencing using a seven-gene MLST scheme can speciate and differentiate Y. enterocolitica biotypes.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | Yersiniosis

items.subtopic: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.assertion: Human plague outbreaks are often preceded by epizootics in rodent populations.
items.reason: Mass deaths among rodents lead to fleas seeking new hosts, causing incidental infection of other mammals.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why human plague outbreaks follow epizootics.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · Plague and Other Yersinia Infections | PLAGUE | ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: The decline in reports of plague from the Democratic Republic of the Congo may not indicate a true decrease.
items.reason: Ongoing conflict in the DRC affects surveillance rather than reflecting an actual reduction in cases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · GLOBAL FEATURES

items.assertion: Primary pneumonic plague is the most common form of plague presentation worldwide.
items.reason: Pneumonic plague was predominant in the 2017 Madagascar outbreak.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because bubonic plague is the most common; however, the reason is true as pneumonic plague was predominant in that specific outbreak.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · GLOBAL FEATURES

items.assertion: The ymt gene is crucial for Y. pestis to colonize and multiply in fleas fed on infected blood.
items.reason: Phospholipase D encoded by the ymt gene is required for this process.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Y. pestis can survive in neutrophils.
items.reason: It avoids being killed by autophagy within macrophages.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; Y. pestis survives and replicates in neutrophils independently of its interaction with macrophages.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Primary pneumonic plague has a short incubation period.
items.reason: It results from inhalation of infectious bacteria in droplets expelled by another person or animal with primary or secondary plague pneumonia.
items.explanation: The short incubation period is due to the direct inhalation route of infection.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.assertion: Septicemic plague without lymphadenopathy occurs in all age groups.
items.reason: Persons >40 years are at elevated risk for septicemic plague.
items.explanation: While persons >40 are at higher risk, septicemic plague can occur across all age groups.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.assertion: Y. pestis can be identified by PCR for specific targets.
items.reason: PCR is one of the reference laboratory tests for definitive identification of Y. pestis isolates.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 1)

items.assertion: Y. pestis forms larger colonies than other Enterobacteriaceae on nutrient agar.
items.reason: Y. pestis grows on standard laboratory media but forms smaller colonies than other Enterobacteriaceae.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; Y. pestis forms smaller, not larger, colonies.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 1)

items.assertion: Detailed phylogeographic DNA sequence data can be used for real-time clinical plague epidemiology.
items.reason: Such an approach has been accumulated to trace plague evolution.
items.explanation: Both are true, but the reason does not directly explain how it could be adapted for real-time use.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · And either of the following (part 2)

items.assertion: Cell-mediated immunity is critical for protection and clearance of Y. pestis from the host.
items.reason: Studies show that neutralizing antibody provides protection against exposure but cell-mediated immunity is crucial for clearance.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 1)

items.assertion: A killed whole-cell vaccine is currently available for preventing pneumonic plague.
items.reason: This vaccine caused significant reactions and was not protective against pneumonic plague.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · ☐ PREVENTION (part 1)

items.assertion: Most clinical infections of Y. enterocolitica are associated with virulence plasmid-containing low-pathogenic strains.
items.reason: These strains belong to four phylogroups corresponding to classical groups of Biotype 2/3 serogroup O:9, BT4 O:3, BT2/3 O:5, and BT5.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association with virulence plasmid-containing strains.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | Yersiniosis

items.assertion: Y. enterocolitica strains lacking a virulence plasmid are generally regarded as nonpathogenic.
items.reason: These strains do not cause infection in mouse models and lack genes associated with Yersinia pathogenesis.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because these strains contain other genes associated with Yersinia pathogenesis and are pathogenic in an insect model.
items.reference: Plague and Other Yersinia Infections · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | Yersiniosis
